From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

New Zealand writer, photographer and community worker

| Qiane Matata-Sipu  [MNZM](/wiki/Member_of_the_New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "Member of the New Zealand Order of Merit") | |
| --- | --- |
| Matata-Sipu in 2023 | |
| Born | 1985 or 1986 (age 39–40)  [Māngere](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere "Māngere"), [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand |
| Occupations | * Artist * journalist * photographer * social activist |
| Spouse | Willie Sipu |
| Website | [www.qiane.co.nz](http://www.qiane.co.nz/) |

**Qiane May Matata-Sipu** [MNZM](/wiki/Member_of_the_New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "Member of the New Zealand Order of Merit") (born 1985 or 1986) is a New Zealand community worker, public speaker, writer, and photographer.

## Early life and family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Qiane_Matata-Sipu&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and family")]

Matata-Sipu was born in 1985 or 1985,[[1]](#cite_note-1) and grew up in [Māngere](/wiki/M%C4%81ngere "Māngere") and [Ihumātao](/wiki/Ihum%C4%81tao "Ihumātao") in Auckland.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) She affiliates to the [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") nations of [Te Wai o Hua ki Te Ahiwaru me Te Ākitai](/wiki/Te_%C4%80kitai_Waiohua "Te Ākitai Waiohua"), [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(iwi) "Waikato (iwi)"), [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") and [Ngāti Pikiao](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Pikiao "Ngāti Pikiao"). Her heritage is also from [Mangaia](/wiki/Mangaia "Mangaia") and from [Arorangi](/wiki/Arorangi "Arorangi") on [Rarotonga](/wiki/Rarotonga "Rarotonga"), both in the Cook Islands. Her surname combines the surnames of her grandfather, Jones Matata, and her husband, Willie Sipu.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Her grandparents raised her and she spent a lot of time at her [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae") with them as a child.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Her grandmother Dawn Matata was a weaver, and her artwork is in the [wharenui](/wiki/Wharenui "Wharenui") (meeting house) at the marae. Her grandfather Jones Matata, was one of the people who carved the wharenui.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Education and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Qiane_Matata-Sipu&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education and career")]

Matata-Sipu has a university degree majoring in journalism from [Auckland University of Technology](/wiki/Auckland_University_of_Technology "Auckland University of Technology") (AUT).[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) She studied *rumaki reo* (total immersion [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language")) at [Te Wānanga Takiura](/w/index.php?title=Te_W%C4%81nanga_Takiura&action=edit&redlink=1 "Te Wānanga Takiura (page does not exist)").[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

As a journalist Matata-Sipu worked for *Mana*, a New Zealand magazine, and was deputy editor of *Spasifik*, a Māori and [Pasifika](/wiki/Pasifika_New_Zealander "Pasifika New Zealander") magazine.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) As a freelance journalist Matata-Sipu has written for New Zealand media *Newsroom*, *[The Spinoff](/wiki/The_Spinoff "The Spinoff")* and *New Zealand Geographic*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[5]](#cite_note-5) She founded a company called QIANE+co in 2017.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

Matata-Sipu is the founder and creator of NUKU, a [multimedia](/wiki/Multimedia "Multimedia") series, in 2018.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) NUKU 'invites wāhine to look at the world through a different cultural lens: one made by and for Indigenous women, mā hine mō hine kia hine'.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) As well as including photography, audio podcast, video, and live events, NUKU has also published a book in 2022 entitled *NUKU: Stories of 100 Indigenous Women*.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 2022 Matata-Sipu was a presenter at M9, an event with [Māori speakers](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"), which is described as part 'TED talk' and part conference.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2022, Matata-Sipu also appeared at the [Verb Wellington](/wiki/Verb_Wellington "Verb Wellington") literary festival.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Matata-Sipu is a co-founder and leader of the SOUL Protect [Ihumātao](/wiki/Ihum%C4%81tao "Ihumātao") campaign, and is a trustee of Makaurau Marae.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)

## Honours and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Qiane_Matata-Sipu&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Honours and awards")]

At the 2021 [New Zealand Women of Influence Awards](/wiki/New_Zealand_Women_of_Influence_Awards "New Zealand Women of Influence Awards"), Matata-Sipu was the winner of the arts and culture category.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) The following year, her first book, *NUKU: Stories of 100 Indigenous Women,* was shortlisted in the illustrated non-fiction category of the [Ockham New Zealand Book Awards](/wiki/Ockham_New_Zealand_Book_Awards "Ockham New Zealand Book Awards").[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)

In the [2023 King's Birthday and Coronation Honours](/wiki/2023_Birthday_and_Coronation_Honours_(New_Zealand) "2023 Birthday and Coronation Honours (New Zealand)"), Matata-Sipu was appointed a [Member of the New Zealand Order of Merit](/wiki/Member_of_the_New_Zealand_Order_of_Merit "Member of the New Zealand Order of Merit"), for services to the arts.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The citation for the honour recorded that she had "contributed significantly to Māori and Pasifika self-determination for 20 years", and that through NUKU, established by her in 2018, she had worked "to celebrate the sovereignty of Indigenous women and reduce impacts of racial discrimination and social exclusion".[[13]](#cite_note-13)
